tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
hint.details:
- Constant unknown stride: 1 occurrence(s)
Non-unit stride (uncontiguous) accesses are not efficiently using data caches
hint.title: Slow data structures access
hint.txt: Detected data structures (typically arrays) that cannot be efficiently read/written

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
 1) align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 32, size); }.
 2) inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 32 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 32) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.
<<image_vec_align>>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 13 optimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.title: Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt:
1 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
53 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 205 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 89: addition or subtraction (16 inside FMA instructions)
 - 76: multiply (16 inside FMA instructions)
 - 36: divide
 - 4: square root
The binary loop is loading 560 bytes (70 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 32 bytes (4 double precision FP elements).

hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.35 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 84.00 cycles. At this rate:
 - 10% of peak load performance is reached (6.67 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 1% of peak store performance is reached (0.38 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.help: List of bottlenecks detected by CQA. A bottleneck is an issue which reduces front-end throughput below the core capacity.
expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks

header: 15% of peak computational performance is used (2.44 out of 16.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.details:
94% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 88% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 94% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
 - 85% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is highly vectorized.
96% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).

gain.workaround:
- Reduce the number of division or square root instructions:
  * If denominator is constant over iterations, use reciprocal (replace x/y with x*(1/y)). Check precision impact. This will be done by your compiler with ffast-math or Ofast
 - Check whether you really need double precision. If not, switch to single precision to speedup execution
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt:
Performance is limited by execution of divide and square root operations (the divide/square root unit is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 84.00 to 27.00 cycles (3.11x speedup).

potential.title: Expensive FP math instructions/calls
potential.txt:
Detected performance impact from expensive FP math instructions/calls.
By removing/reexpressing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 84.00 to 27.00 cycles (3.11x speedup).

potential.workaround:
Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt:
Detected 16 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

hint.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
